title: Fatigue and body aches
summary: Fatigue and muscle aches are common in influenza and other viral infections. The main aims are pain relief, hydration, sleep and a gradual return to activity rather than pushing through or remaining completely inactive for days.
evidenceSnapshot: Paracetamol and NSAIDs help pain and fever. Evidence that supplements or special foods rapidly restore energy is poor.

interventions.name: Sleep and reduced workload
interventions.finding: Supports recovery and reduces the chance of overexertion when acutely unwell.
interventions.practical: Cancel non-essential tasks, take brief naps if needed, and protect a full night of sleep.
interventions.mechanism: Sleep supports immune regulation and pain tolerance.
interventions.cautions: Prolonged bed rest can increase stiffness and deconditioning.

interventions.name: Paracetamol or ibuprofen
interventions.finding: Reduces headache, muscle aches and fever-related discomfort.
interventions.practical: Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest time and follow the label.
interventions.cautions: See medicine cautions in the OTC module.

interventions.name: Gentle movement when able
interventions.finding: Short walks around the home and stretching can reduce stiffness after the worst phase has passed.
interventions.practical: Stop if dizzy, breathless, faint or significantly worse afterward.
interventions.cautions: Avoid vigorous exercise with fever, chest symptoms or marked systemic illness.

interventions.name: Energy drinks and 'immune boosters'
interventions.finding: Do not treat viral fatigue; high caffeine or sugar may worsen sleep, palpitations or dehydration.

practicalSteps: Use a simple rhythm: drink, eat a little, rest, briefly move, then reassess.
Return to exercise gradually after fever has resolved and energy is clearly improving.
Expect influenza fatigue to last longer than a mild cold.
avoidOrCare: New weakness affecting one side, severe muscle pain with dark urine, fainting or chest pain is not routine viral fatigue.
Persistent exhaustion after the acute illness may need review for complications or another diagnosis.

myths.claim: Exercise will sweat the virus out.
myths.reality: Hard exercise during fever or systemic illness adds physiological stress and may worsen symptoms.

myths.claim: Sugar or vitamins provide instant immune energy.
myths.reality: Correcting a deficiency matters; megadoses during an ordinary cold rarely produce a rapid clinical effect.

redFlags: Chest pain, fainting or severe shortness of breath
Severe weakness, confusion or inability to stay awake
Dark urine with severe muscle pain
unsettled: The best graded return-to-exercise protocol after uncomplicated influenza has limited direct trial evidence.
